songs.title: Blocked and Deleted
songs.description: A witty, modern break-up anthem that turns the final act of moving on into an empowering declaration of independence.
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★★ — Strong contemporary concept with immediate commercial appeal and an instantly memorable title.
songs.publisherNotes: One of the strongest concept-driven songs in the current catalogue. Excellent candidate for publisher presentations and one of the first songs to introduce to new visitors. The lyric demonstrates a distinctive Marveltonez strength: taking an everyday modern experience and turning it into an engaging pop narrative.

profile.overview: Relationships don’t always end with dramatic confrontations—sometimes they end with a tap on a screen. Blocked and Deleted captures that moment with humour, confidence and a contemporary edge, transforming a familiar digital-age experience into an instantly relatable pop concept. Rather than dwelling on heartbreak, the song celebrates taking control, drawing a line under the past and embracing a fresh start. Its conversational lyric and memorable central phrase make it immediately accessible, while giving the song a distinctive identity within today’s pop landscape.
profile.themes: Modern relationships • Break-ups • Self-respect • Digital communication • Moving on • Emotional independence
profile.musicalStyle: A contemporary commercial pop song built around a memorable lyrical hook and a conversational storytelling style. The emphasis is on personality, relatable situations and a modern perspective rather than melodrama, making it suitable for artists looking for confident, character-driven material.
profile.artistComparisons: Dua Lipa • Anne-Marie • Maisie Peters • Sabrina Carpenter • Olivia Rodrigo
profile.perfectFor: Break-up playlists • Empowerment playlists • Girls’ night playlists • Modern pop playlists • Driving playlists • New beginnings playlists
profile.syncPotential: Romantic comedy • Streaming drama • Teen/YA television • Lifestyle programming • Fashion or beauty campaigns • Social media marketing

songs.title: Put Your Shirt Back On
songs.description: A sharp, chaotic, panic-the-morning-after pop story in which one spectacularly bad decision turns into an urgent race for the door.
songs.mood: Chaotic • Cheeky • Frantic • Mischievous • Regretful • Bold
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★☆ — A highly memorable title and vivid, cinematic premise give it strong commercial and sync appeal, although the explicit language and infidelity storyline narrow its suitability for some mainstream radio, family-facing brands and public playlists.
songs.publisherNotes: One of the catalogue’s clearest visual concepts and a strong candidate for adult comedy or dramedy sync. The title lands immediately and the lyric creates a complete scene. A clean alternative lyric or radio edit would widen pitching options without replacing the original version.

profile.overview: Put Your Shirt Back On drops the listener straight into the panic of a drunken mistake as the singer panics and scrambles to find her clothes, her keys and any convincing escape route. The song turns embarrassment, temptation and instant regret into a fast-moving scene, driven by an unmistakable title hook “Put your shirt back on”. Beneath the chaos sits a clear sense of consequence: the singer knows the situation is reckless and wants to get out of there before it becomes an even bigger disaster. Bold, visual and unapologetically adult, it brings a mischievous edge to the Marveltonez catalogue.
profile.themes: Drunken mistakes • Infidelity • Panic and escape • Consequences • Temptation • Morning-after regret
profile.musicalStyle: A concept-led contemporary pop song built around rapid-fire conversational storytelling, comic escalation and a highly repeatable title hook “Put your shirt back on”. Its appeal lies in character, timing and a scene that unfolds almost like a short film, making it especially suited to an artist comfortable with humour, attitude and adult subject matter.
profile.artistComparisons: Lily Allen • Sabrina Carpenter • Anne-Marie • Chappell Roan • Katy Perry
profile.perfectFor: Girls’ night playlists • Cheeky pop playlists • Adult pop playlists • Party aftermath playlists • Comedy and character-driven pop • Explicit pop playlists
profile.syncPotential: Adult romantic comedy • Streaming dramedy • Nightlife or party montage • Comedy series • Dating-show promo • Fashion or beauty content with a mischievous tone

songs.title: So You’re Dating My Ex
songs.description: A sharp, tongue-in-cheek pop anthem that flips jealousy on its head with confidence, humour.. and the last laugh.
songs.genre: Contemporary Pop / Commercial Pop / Attitude Pop
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★★ — A memorable title, instantly understandable concept and fresh emotional perspective combine to create strong commercial potential.
songs.publisherNotes: One of the strongest examples of the Marveltonez writing style. The lyric balances humour, confidence and emotional maturity without becoming bitter. Excellent publisher showcase material.

profile.overview: So You’re Dating My Ex takes a situation that usually fuels drama and turns it into a witty display of confidence and emotional independence. Rather than dwelling on resentment, the singer refuses to play the expected role, and meets rivalry with sarcasm, humour and complete indifference. Packed with memorable one-liners and a strong conversational style, the song captures the confidence of a girl who has already moved on, making it both relatable and entertaining. Its bold title, playful attitude and contemporary language give it immediate commercial appeal while reinforcing the Marveltonez catalogue’s knack for turning everyday relationship situations into distinctive pop concepts.
profile.themes: Moving on • Modern relationships • Emotional confidence • Rivalry • Self-worth • Humour after heartbreak
profile.musicalStyle: A contemporary pop song driven by conversational storytelling, memorable lyrical hooks and a confident vocal attitude. Rather than relying on emotional drama, it builds its appeal through wit, personality and a fresh perspective on a familiar relationship scenario.
profile.artistComparisons: Sabrina Carpenter • Olivia Rodrigo • Maisie Peters • Anne-Marie • Lily Allen
profile.perfectFor: Break-up playlists • Feel-good pop playlists • Girls’ night playlists • Empowerment playlists • Road trips • Contemporary pop playlists
profile.syncPotential: Romantic comedy • Teen/young adult drama • Streaming comedy • Lifestyle programming • Fashion campaigns • Social media content • Relationship montages

songs.title: Just Sayin’
songs.description: A barbed, comic-pop takedown of polished perfection, delivered with eye-rolls and attitude.
songs.genre: Contemporary Pop / Satirical Pop / Attitude Pop
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★☆ — Strong personality, a highly reusable hook phrase and vivid lyric writing give it commercial appeal; the abrasive language and intentionally judgmental character make it more targeted than universal.
songs.publisherNotes: A strong showcase for the sharper, more satirical side of the catalogue. The narrator’s hypocrisy is part of the humour, but positioning and artist choice will be important. A clean version would broaden radio and sync options.

profile.overview: Just Sayin’ gives the singer a free rein to puncture the image of those carefully cultivated “bubble-gum girls,” prima-donna egos and high-maintenance perfection. The lyric is knowingly contradictory: she insists she could never be jealous, while cataloguing everything that irritates her, and that self-awareness is part of the joke. Packed with vivid put-downs, conversational profanity and the endlessly reusable title phrase, the song creates a bold character rather than a neutral observer. Its satirical edge and repeatable hook make it a distinctive, mischievous entry in the Marveltonez catalogue.
profile.themes: Jealousy • Image and vanity • Social rivalry • Self-awareness • Female competition • Attitude
profile.musicalStyle: A character-driven contemporary pop song built around rhythmic verbal hooks, comic exaggeration and an unapologetically sarcastic point of view. The title “Just Sayin’” acts as both punchline and escape clause, giving the lyric a strong identity.
profile.artistComparisons: Lily Allen • Sabrina Carpenter • Chappell Roan • Anne-Marie • Meghan Trainor
profile.perfectFor: Attitude-pop playlists • Girls’ night playlists • Satirical pop • Workout playlists • Character-driven pop • Explicit pop playlists
profile.syncPotential: Comedy series • Reality television • Fashion satire • Social-media campaigns • Teen/young-adult dramedy • Character-introduction montage

songs.title: I Didn’t Mean to Turn Out Bad
songs.description: A rebellious yet vulnerable pop confession, from a girl refusing to fit back inside the box her family imagined for her.
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★★ — The title is memorable, the family address is immediate and the central conflict is widely relatable. Strong artist-pitch potential, provided the production supports both its bite and vulnerability.
songs.publisherNotes: One of the catalogue’s strongest emotional concepts. The “candy in a box” image and direct appeal to Mum and Dad provide a clear identity. Worth prioritising for an artist who can sell both rebellion and hurt.

profile.overview: I Didn’t Mean to Turn Out Bad blends apology, anger and self-defence as the singer tries to explain the person she has become. The recurring image of “candy in a box” captures the pressure to remain sweet, compliant and easy to consume, while the verses reveal a messier life of bad choices, judgment and limited options. The title “I Didn’t Mean to Turn Out Bad” is emotionally potent because it can sound both defiant and wounded at the same time. Contemporary language and flashes of humour keep the song from becoming self-pitying, giving it the feel of a coming-of-age anthem with genuine character.
profile.themes: Family expectations • Identity • Rebellion • Judgment • Growing up • Self-acceptance
profile.musicalStyle: A dramatic contemporary pop song built around a strong title and the contrast between sweetness expected and chaos lived. It suits an artist able to combine attitude with emotional exposure.
profile.artistComparisons: Olivia Rodrigo • Gayle • Anne-Marie • Maisie Peters • Avril Lavigne
profile.perfectFor: Coming-of-age playlists • Rebellion playlists • Emotional pop-rock • Youth anthems • Empowerment playlists • Cathartic listening
profile.syncPotential: Teen drama • Coming-of-age film • Family-conflict montage • Youth campaign • Character-breakthrough scene • Streaming drama

songs.title: It’s a Happy Break-Up
songs.description: A stomping break-up anthem that swaps tears for make-up, relationships for freedom and the pleasure of rediscovering yourself.
songs.genre: Contemporary Pop / Feel-Good Pop / Break-Up Pop
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★★ — The title communicates the concept instantly, the chorus is highly marketable and the positive viewpoint creates strong streaming and sync possibilities.
songs.publisherNotes: A particularly clear publisher pitch because the idea is understood from the title alone. Strong candidate for upbeat artist pitching and celebratory sync. The lyric offers specific character details that help it feel more than a generic empowerment song.

profile.overview: It’s a Happy Break-Up overturns the expected sadness of separation by presenting its singer as relieved, energised and newly curious about her own life. She remembers neglected interests, reconnects with friends and enjoys the freedom to dance, drink and party. The chorus is built on a simple, memorable rhyme—“It’s a happy break-up / putting on my make-up”—and the repeated list of everything now out of her life gives the song a celebratory release. A brief moment of wondering whether her ex misses her or not adds an emotional texture without disturbing the song’s upbeat centre.
profile.themes: Freedom • Rediscovery • Moving on • Friendship • Neglect within relationships • New beginnings
profile.musicalStyle: A feel-good contemporary pop song with a clear singalong chorus, conversational verses and an optimistic reversal of the traditional break-up narrative. Its strength lies in the contrast between what others expect her to feel and the freedom she actually experiences in her break-up.
profile.artistComparisons: Dua Lipa • Anne-Marie • Meghan Trainor • Katy Perry • Jess Glynne
profile.perfectFor: Break-up recovery • Girls’ night playlists • Feel-good pop • Fresh-start playlists • Getting-ready playlists • Driving playlists
profile.syncPotential: Romantic comedy • Makeover or getting-ready montage • Lifestyle advertising • Reality TV • Travel or nightlife content • Fresh-start campaign

songs.title: Doorstep
songs.description: A sweetly awkward pop story about the moment a perfect date reaches the front door—but the expected kiss never comes.
songs.genre: Contemporary Pop / Romantic Pop / Story Pop
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★☆ — The premise is universal, visual and charming, with strong sync potential. Commercial impact will depend on the melodic payoff of the doorstep chorus and the production’s ability to build anticipation.
songs.publisherNotes: A highly visual and relatable concept that provides welcome softness and romantic awkwardness within the catalogue. Strong sync candidate for scenes where a hoped-for kiss fails to happen.

profile.overview: Doorstep captures the tension of two people who clearly get along, but repeatedly fail to cross the final romantic threshold. Each drive home raises the singer’s hopes, only for silence, politeness and another disappearing car to leave her wondering what she said, what she missed and whether or not this was supposed to be the moment they kissed. The humour comes from her outward coolness colliding with the increasingly frustrated thoughts in her head. Built around a familiar, highly visual situation, the song turns romantic uncertainty into a concise and relatable pop narrative.
profile.themes: Dating uncertainty • Missed signals • Romantic anticipation • Shyness • Miscommunication • Unrequited momentum
profile.musicalStyle: A narrative contemporary pop song driven by escalating anticipation and a chorus that freezes the action at the doorstep. Its appeal lies in recognisable detail, emotional restraint and the comic gap between what the singer expects, and what actually happens.
profile.artistComparisons: Maisie Peters • Carly Rae Jepsen • Sabrina Carpenter • Gabrielle Aplin • Anne-Marie
profile.perfectFor: Dating playlists • Romantic-pop playlists • Crush playlists • Road-trip pop • Lighthearted relationship playlists • Story-song collections
profile.syncPotential: Romantic comedy • First-date montage • Teen/young-adult drama • Dating-show sequence • Sitcom end scene • Awkward-romance advertisement

songs.title: A Girl Like Me
songs.description: A warm, melody-led love song about hearing a song and realising its words seem written for you.
songs.genre: Contemporary Pop / Retro-Influenced Pop / Romantic Pop
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★☆ — The lyric has warmth, a clear title payoff and strong melodic signposts. Its commercial strength is likely to depend heavily on the recording’s melody and production character.
songs.publisherNotes: A useful contrast to the catalogue’s sharper relationship songs. The “nothing but everything” line and the song-within-a-song concept are distinctive. Potentially strong for an artist with a warm, melodic identity.

profile.overview: A Girl Like Me begins with a song drifting into the street as it pulls the narrator into its melody. As she listens, she hears something personal in the words—as if the singer of the song she is hearing is singing directly to her. The lyric moves from fascination to love, using recurring “sha-la-la” and “oh-oh” refrains to frame a simple romantic idea: she does not need diamonds or grand displays, only the “nothing but everything” in the song he has to give. Gentle, affectionate and knowingly classic in its language, the song adds a melodic, timeless side to the Marveltonez catalogue.
profile.themes: Music as connection • Falling in love • Simplicity over materialism • Recognition • Melody • Emotional openness
profile.musicalStyle: A melodic pop song with classic, repeated title phrases and a romantic narrative centred on the emotional power of hearing a song and feeling it could have been written for you. Its language suggests a light retro-pop vibe while remaining accessible to a contemporary audience.
profile.artistComparisons: Carly Rae Jepsen • Kylie Minogue • Meghan Trainor • Jess Glynne • Paloma Faith
profile.perfectFor: Feel-good romance playlists • Easy listening • Summer playlists • Retro-pop playlists • Love-song collections • Daytime listening
profile.syncPotential: Romantic comedy • Music-themed film or series • Street-scene montage • Lifestyle advertising • Feel-good drama • Artist-performance sequence

songs.title: You’re My Boy
songs.description: A bright, open-hearted pop celebration of a new love so exciting that it has to be shared with the whole world.
songs.genre: Contemporary Pop / Feel-Good Pop / Romantic Pop
songs.commercialPotential: ★★★★☆ — The message is universal and immediately accessible, with good playlist and sync value. Its commercial distinctiveness will depend more on melody, vocal character and production than on lyrical complexity.
songs.publisherNotes: A straightforward, positive love song that rounds out the catalogue emotionally. Useful for sync briefs requiring uncomplicated joy and for artists seeking an open, singalong romantic track.

profile.overview: You’re My Boy captures the uncomplicated rush of falling in love, and wanting everyone to know. The singer feels the rush of love so physically and emotionally that she can’t help but phone her friends and throw her hands into the air as a public declaration. The lyric’s directness is its defining quality: this is not a complicated relationship story, but a burst of joy, happiness and anticipation. Repeated “love, love, love” phrases and the central “whole wide world” images make it naturally suited to a big chorus and give the song an upbeat and affectionate place in the catalogue.
profile.themes: New love • Public celebration • Attraction • Emotional security • Excitement • Togetherness
profile.musicalStyle: An upbeat contemporary pop song built around direct emotional language and an uplifting chorus designed as a joyful public announcement. Its simplicity leaves space for a strong melody, with an energetic arrangement.
profile.artistComparisons: Carly Rae Jepsen • Jess Glynne • Kylie Minogue • Meghan Trainor • Katy Perry
profile.perfectFor: New-love playlists • Feel-good pop • Celebration playlists • Summer driving • Wedding-party playlists • Upbeat romance
profile.syncPotential: Romantic comedy • New-couple montage • Wedding or engagement content • Family/lifestyle advertising • Feel-good television • Celebration campaign
